claim_text: The canonical audit behavior is defined in `workflows/claim_audit.yml` and executed through the shared runtime contract in `docs/runtime_contract.md`.
Output quality is expected to vary; the current goal is to demonstrate the workflow contract and evidence-bounded report structure.
It does not decide whether a project is good; it records what the selected repository evidence can and cannot support.
source_location: section: ClaimLint > What it solves; line 13

claim_text: Classifies claim types and required evidence.
source_location: section: ClaimLint > What v0.1 does; line 20
section: ClaimLint > What v0.1 does not do; line 25

claim_text: It is not a legal, compliance, scientific, or safety certification tool.
source_location: section: ClaimLint > What v0.1 does not do; line 27

claim_text: It does not run a GitHub bot, write pull requests, crawl arbitrary repositories outside the manifest, use a hosted model API, run local open-weight models, fine-tune models, or claim exhaustive repository auditing.
source_location: section: ClaimLint > What v0.1 does not do; line 27

claim_text: Adapters point to that workflow and the shared runtime contract; they do not redefine either one.
A README metric claim may be `partially_supported` when a matching metric file exists, but an evaluation command or released checkpoint hash is missing.
Future work may add evaluated local backends, richer retrieval, benchmark evaluation, and LoRA-related experiments.
Those are not part of v0.1.
ClaimLint v0.1 is organized around four separate layers.
`docs/runtime_contract.md` defines the shared CLI surface and required output files.
Adapter files point to the workflow and runtime contract.
They must not redefine the workflow or invent a different CLI.
`src/claimlint/` implements the runtime contract with deterministic ingestion, extraction, retrieval, judgement, schema validation, and rendering.
v0.1 includes a model backend abstraction but does not depend on hosted or local model inference.
